artifacts.content:
From: michael.oconnor@gmail.com
To: mortgage.arrears.team@greenislebank.ie
Date: 2025-02-11 09:18
Subject: Re: Request for updated income & background details – O’Connor Account

Dear Aoife,

Thanks for your patience while I pulled everything together. I’ve attached the completed income and expenditure form for myself and my wife, Emma Louise O’Connor, along with the supporting payslips and bank statements you requested.

You asked me to confirm Emma’s details where the system wouldn’t accept my earlier entry. Her date of birth is 1985-04-29, so the day is 29.0, the month 04 and the year 1985. She’s a full‑time primary school teacher at St. Brigid’s National School in Castleknock.

By way of explanation for how our arrears built up: our situation worsened after my younger brother, Sean O’Connor, was involved in a serious car accident near Galway University Hospital on 12 June 2024. I took multiple unpaid days off in June and July and transferred a total of €2,750 from our joint account to help him with rent and rehab costs. That left us short and we couldn’t cover the full July and August mortgage payments.

I also need to hold my hands up and admit that I kept assuming I’d get a bonus or a pay rise to catch up the missed payments, so I didn’t contact you early on when I first fell behind. In hindsight that was a mistake, and I’m trying to be fully transparent now so we can agree a realistic arrangement.

If anything in the form isn’t clear, you can reach me on 08X XXX XXXX during lunch (12:30–13:30) or after 5 p.m.

Kind regards,

Michael Andrew O’Connor
Senior Network Engineer, DigitalConnect Solutions Ltd.
Willowbank Drive, Castleknock, Dublin 15

metadata.subject: Re: Request for updated income & background details – O’Connor Account

artifacts.content:
From: Emma O'Connor <emma.oconnor@example.com>
To: Michael O'Connor <m.oconnor@example.com>
Date: 2024-02-18 21:07
Subject: Forms for mortgage review – can you double check?

Hi love,

I’ve just gone through the updated mortgage review pack they emailed over this afternoon. There’s a section asking for “Borrower 2 date of birth – day / month / year” and I wasn’t sure what exactly they wanted me to put where.

For mine I’ve written the day as 14, month as 06 and year as 1985 on the rough copy, but I’ll leave the final version until you’ve checked it. The layout is confusing – three tiny boxes side by side, and I don’t want to mix the day and month the way you did once on that Ryanair booking.

They also want an explanation again about why we fell into arrears last autumn. I’ve jotted down the thing about your overtime being cut and the hospital bits with Liam, but I completely forgot the bit about you having to pay €1,150 in back child maintenance to the Child Maintenance Service in the UK on 10 October 2023. You said that came due all at once because of some administrative error from that short relationship before we met, and that you had to pull it out of the mortgage overpayment pot in the AIB account, which meant we couldn’t make the higher payment that month.

Can you edit what I’ve written and fill in the date-of-birth day boxes properly before I scan and send everything on Thursday?

E x

metadata.subject: Forms for mortgage review – can you double check?

artifacts.content:
From: Michael O'Connor <m.oconnor@example.com>
To: Sarah Kavanagh <sarah.kavanagh@mortgageassist.ie>
Date: 2024-02-21 10:42
Subject: Re: Missing info on affordability questionnaire

Hi Sarah,

Thanks for flagging the gaps on the affordability questionnaire.

On the point about Emma’s details (she’s the second borrower), I saw the note that the “borrower 2 date of birth – day” box was left blank on the scanned copy. That was my fault – I was halfway through the form when I had to jump on a client call about a VPN failure and never came back to that page before we dropped the envelope into your office.

For reference, I know you already have her age from the original application (she’s 39 at the moment and will turn 40 this summer), and her full name matches the payslips from St. Brigid’s National School. I’ll get her to handwrite the day clearly in black pen when she’s home from school and then I’ll send you a fresh scan of just that page so you don’t have to re-key everything.

I’ll also attach a short note explaining the October arrears. That includes the unexpected €1,150 payment I had to make to the Child Maintenance Service in the UK on 10 October 2023 after an administrative error on their side. I transferred it from our AIB mortgage overpayment pot, which meant we couldn’t make the usual higher payment that month.

Let me know if you need anything else.

Regards,
Michael

metadata.subject: Re: Missing info on affordability questionnaire

artifacts.content:
Title: Finish mortgage paperwork – Emma’s DOB + arrears note

Location: Kitchen table
Attendees: Michael, Emma

Agenda / Notes:

- Sit down with Emma after kids are in bed to finish the last bits of the mortgage review pack.
- Page 3: double-check the personal details section for both borrowers. Emma’s bit is labelled “Borrower 2”. Need to:
  * Fill in the small box that asks for “date of birth – day” (I left it blank on the last version).
  * Confirm the month and year match what’s on her passport and the HR records from St. Brigid’s.
- Make sure the handwriting is very clear this time – last time the adviser said the scanner made the tiny boxes hard to read and it looked like a 7 or a 1.
- Go through the arrears explanation section together:
  * Note the reduced overtime from DigitalConnect from August onwards.
  * Mention Emma dropping to 3.5 days a week for Liam’s assessments and asthma appointments.
  * Add the bit about the €1,150 back child maintenance I had to pay to the Child Maintenance Service in the UK on 10 October 2023. That came out of the AIB mortgage overpayment pot, which is why we couldn’t do the higher payment that month.
- Aim to scan and email everything to the broker first thing tomorrow.

metadata.title: Finish mortgage paperwork – Emma’s DOB + arrears note

artifacts.content:
From: Sarah Kavanagh <sarah.kavanagh@mortgageassist.ie>
To: Michael O'Connor <m.oconnor@example.com>
Date: 2024-02-23 09:16
Subject: Clarification on Emma’s details (Borrower 2)

Hi Michael,

Thanks for sending through the updated scan last night. Most of the missing information is now in place, but there are still two small issues relating to Emma’s section as Borrower 2.

1. In the personal details table, the box labelled “Date of Birth – Day” is still not entirely clear. It looks like the pen skipped slightly when Emma was writing, so on the system it has come through as a faint line rather than a definite number. Could you please reply to this email simply confirming the numeric day of her birth (e.g. “day = 03” or similar) so I can type it directly into the lender portal? You don’t need to resend the whole page again.

2. In the arrears explanation, you mentioned the reduced overtime and Emma’s temporary reduction in hours, which is great. The underwriter may also query the one-off payment you referenced for child maintenance in October 2023. I gather you had to pay €1,150 to the Child Maintenance Service in the UK on 10 October due to an administrative error, and that you took this from your AIB mortgage overpayment pot. Could you confirm in writing that this was a one-time situation linked to a historic relationship and that there are no ongoing obligations?

Once I have the exact day of birth for Emma and that clarification, we should be ready to submit.

Kind regards,
Sarah

metadata.subject: Clarification on Emma’s details (Borrower 2)

artifacts.content:

Date: 2024-02-27 12:00-12:30
Location: Quiet meeting room, DigitalConnect Solutions
Attendees: Michael; Aoife (case officer, lender)

Description:

Phone appointment arranged by the lender to tidy up outstanding queries on the joint mortgage review.

Talking points / reminders for Michael:

- Have Emma’s passport and driving licence beside you so you can read out her details as Borrower 2.
- They specifically want you to confirm the numeric day for her date of birth because the handwriting on the scanned form was too faint. Make sure you say the number clearly (e.g. “the day is zero-eight”) and check they repeat it back correctly before moving on.
- Verify that the month and year match what’s already on their system from the original application.
- Be ready to explain the October 2023 arrears again:
  * Reduced overtime from August onwards.
  * Emma cutting back to 3.5 days a week for Liam’s assessments and asthma checks.
  * The €1,150 lump-sum payment you had to make to the Child Maintenance Service in the UK on 10 October 2023 because of an administrative error. You took it from the AIB mortgage overpayment pot, which meant you couldn’t make the higher mortgage payment that month.
- Ask them to email written confirmation once they’ve updated the date-of-birth day field and marked the arrears issue as satisfactorily explained.

artifacts.content:
From: brian.kelleher@kellehermortgages.ie
To: michael.oconnor@example.com
Date: 2025-02-18 16:02
Subject: Re: Clarifications on Arrears Support Form

Hi Michael,

Good to speak with you earlier.

Just to follow up on your questions:

1. For the “borrower 2 date of birth day” box, the lender only needs the day of the month expressed as a number (e.g. 02, 11, 17 etc.). They already hold Emma’s full date of birth from the original application, so you don’t need to rewrite the entire date, only the day. Please make sure it matches what they have on file, but don’t worry about including the month or year on this particular line.

2. On the employment section, it’s correct to state that Emma is currently unemployed and to outline that she previously worked full-time as a 3rd class primary school teacher at St. Brigid’s National School, Castleknock, from August 2010 to June 2024. The reference to her being signed off on stress leave by Dr. Fiona Kelly for 12 weeks in early 2024 is useful context, but keep it factual and brief.

3. Regarding the arrears explanation, you can absolutely note that when your 2016 Skoda Octavia broke down and needed the clutch and timing belt replacement, you chose to pay the €1,050 repair bill instead of making the full November mortgage instalment so Emma could still get to the school and Liam’s hospital appointments.

Send the updated pack when ready and I’ll review before it goes to the bank.

Best,
Brian

metadata.subject: Re: Clarifications on Arrears Support Form

artifacts.content:
From: Michael O'Connor <m.oconnor@gmail.com>
To: Customer Services <info@greenleafcredit.ie>
Date: 2024-07-09 10:12
Subject: Clarification on Correspondence Address

Hi Aoife,

Following up on our call yesterday about the mortgage review pack, I just wanted to confirm that post shouldn’t be going to my parents’ place anymore.

For years we used Mam and Dad’s house in Glasnevin as a safe mailing point while we were renting. A lot of older bank letters still show their address on **18 Hollybank Road**, Glasnevin, Dublin 11. That’s probably why your system still has Hollybank in there somewhere.

Since we bought in Castleknock back in 2016, all day‑to‑day stuff comes directly to our own place, and anything important from Revenue or the credit union goes there too. The only reason Hollybank was still popping up on forms was that, during the period between March and June 2024 when we fell into arrears, I was scrambling and re‑using old templates. That was when Emma had cut her hours in St. Brigid’s to attend weekly CBT sessions in Beaumont Hospital’s mental health unit every Thursday afternoon for severe postnatal depression that resurfaced after Liam turned six, and I was paying €95 a session plus extra childcare.

Anyway, to avoid any more confusion or delays, please remove **18 Hollybank Road** from your records as my correspondence address. I’ll write the correct Castleknock address clearly on the enclosed form you’re sending out, but I didn’t want anyone defaulting back to my parents’ place.

Best,
Michael

metadata.subject: Clarification on Correspondence Address

artifacts.content:
From: HR Department <hr@digitalconnect.ie>
To: Michael O'Connor <m.oconnor@digitalconnect.ie>
Date: 2024-02-16 15:47
Subject: Address on File & Contractual Matters

Hi Michael,

As discussed, we’re updating employee records ahead of the next audit. Our system still lists your personal correspondence address as **Flat 4, 12 Parkgate View, Dublin 8**, which you gave us when you joined DigitalConnect Solutions back in May 2019.

Please confirm if **Flat 4, 12 Parkgate View** is still the correct address for all non‑payroll correspondence (e.g. P60 copies, pension statements, etc.). If you’ve moved to your own place in Castleknock permanently, we’ll need the new first line of your home address and Eircode so we can update the HR and payroll databases before the end of the month.

On a separate note, I’m obliged to remind you of the email Brian Kavanagh sent in November 2023 about cash‑in‑hand weekend network installs you were doing for a pub in Blanchardstown. As Brian mentioned, that kind of work may breach the conflict‑of‑interest clause in your contract as a Senior Network Engineer at DigitalConnect Solutions Ltd. in Park West Business Park, Dublin 12, particularly if it involves client‑type infrastructure.

This doesn’t affect your current employment status, but compliance have asked us to ensure your file reflects that the matter was raised and that you were advised to cease that activity.

When replying, please:
1. Confirm your current correspondence address (first line and Eircode at minimum)
2. Confirm you’ve read and understood the conflict‑of‑interest reminder

Kind regards,

Sarah McKenna
HR Generalist
DigitalConnect Solutions Ltd.

For previous occupation, I’ve put: “Senior Network Engineer at DigitalConnect Solutions Ltd., Park West Business Park, Dublin 12, until mutual termination in January 2024.” Just to give context in case underwriting asks: I left under a mutual termination package after HR opened a formal investigation into my remote access logs when a junior engineer on my team, Mark Doyle, misused an admin account I’d left logged in during an overnight maintenance window. There was no Garda involvement, but I know it may raise questions, so better to be upfront.

If you need any supporting documentation (P45, termination letter, or Emma’s latest payslips from St. Brigid’s), I can scan and send them this evening once the kids are in bed.

Give me a shout if anything above needs to be reworded for the underwriters.

Kind regards,

Michael Andrew O’Connor

artifacts.content:
Hi Michael,

Thanks for your message earlier and for confirming Emma’s details. I’ve updated our file to show her as Borrower 2 on the account.

For the purposes of the arrears support review, we need a clear correspondence address for each borrower. From what you said on the phone, it sounds like Emma would prefer that all letters for her are sent care of her school rather than to the house, just in case anything lands on the mat when she’s not there.

Can you confirm that we should use the following for Borrower 2’s correspondence address line 3:

St. Brigid’s National School
Castleknock Village
Dublin 15

If this is not correct, please reply with the exact wording you’d like us to use for line 3 (for example, some customers prefer to include the school name or “c/o” on a separate line). Once we have that, we’ll update our systems and reissue the paperwork.

You also mentioned during the call that you’d been reluctant to open some of the earlier letters about the arrears because it all felt overwhelming. Just to reassure you, you’re not the first person to say that. Many customers tell us something similar – that they took far too long to properly look at their options because they were embarrassed about the arrears and kept putting off dealing with it until the situation had clearly got much worse.

Kind regards,

Sarah Kavanagh
Arrears Support Unit
Emerald Home Loans

metadata.title: Mortgage Arrears Review – Address & Contact Preferences
metadata.location: Phone Appointment – Emerald Home Loans Call Centre

artifacts.content:
Hi Emma,

Just following up on the HR form I left in your pigeonhole yesterday about updating your contact and emergency details. We’re trying to tidy up the staff database before the end of term, and I noticed that the section for your “correspondence address” is blank for line 3.

At the moment, the system shows:

Line 1: c/o St. Brigid’s National School
Line 2: Castleknock
Line 3: [blank]

For payroll and any official letters (e.g. references, parental complaints, Board of Management correspondence), we need something in that third line – even if it’s just a repeat of the locality. A lot of staff in the area simply use “Castleknock, Dublin” or “Castleknock, Dublin 15 area” on line 3, especially if they don’t want their full home address listed in the system.

Could you email me back with exactly what you’d like to appear on line 3 for your correspondence address? Once I have that, I’ll update the system and you won’t be bothered about it again.

Separately, Michael mentioned in passing at the parent–teacher meetings that you’ve had a rough few months with mortgage arrears and that he felt he left it too long to talk to the bank because he was mortified. Please don’t worry – you’re definitely not the only family juggling things at the moment, and if you ever need a letter from the school confirming your hours or role for the bank, just ask.

Best,

Siobhán
School Secretary
St. Brigid’s National School, Castleknock

Morning reminder to sit down with Emma before school run and double-check the personal details for the mortgage arrears review pack.

Agenda:
- Confirm Emma’s date of birth **month** for the “Borrower 2” section – I always mix up whether her birthday is in April or May because Aoife’s party is usually around the May bank holiday and it throws me. Pretty sure Emma’s is early April but need her to write the month in herself on the form so I don’t accidentally put 5 instead of 4.
- Make sure day/year match what’s on her passport and the health insurance records.
- Re-read the section where I explained that arrears started after her certified sick leave from January–April 2024 when she was on half-rate sick pay due to the postnatal depression diagnosis; check that the wording sounds okay and not overly dramatic.
- Scan Emma’s last payslip and the GP letter from Dr. Keane.
- Pack everything into the brown envelope so Emma can post it after school.

Note: Don’t forget to mention in the covering letter that some of the arrears also built up because we prioritised other bills and kids’ appointments over the mortgage, assuming we’d catch up once things settled down.

From: Bríd O'Connor <brid.oconnor@example.com>
To: Michael O'Connor <m.oconnor@gmail.com>
Date: 2024-03-20 15:47
Subject: Re: Your dad’s form and Emma’s birthday month

Hi Michael,

I found your dad’s old pension paperwork and there’s a sample section where they’d filled in spouse details. It reminded me of what you were asking on Sunday about Emma’s birthday month for that bank form.

You were wondering if her birthday was in June because you always associate it with the end of the school year. It isn’t – that’s when Aoife usually has her class show and the GAA blitz. Emma’s birthday is earlier in the year, in **April**, just after the Easter break most years. I remember clearly because she was still on maternity leave with Liam that April and we brought a cake over to the house in Castleknock.

Anyway, the pension sample shows how they wrote the month as just the number (e.g. 4 for April, 6 for June), so for the “Borrower 2 date of birth month” line you mentioned, you probably just need to put the single digit and not 04. But let Emma write it in herself to be safe; you don’t want to have to redo the whole arrears review if there’s a mistake.

Hope that helps. Give my love to Emma and the kids.

Mam

From: Michael O'Connor <m.oconnor@gmail.com>

Date: 2024-07-02 21:14
Subject: Forms for the arrears review – need your DOB

Hi Em,

I’ve started filling in that arrears review pack they sent out and I need to double‑check a few bits about you before I send it back.

They’re asking for “Borrower 2 date of birth (month and year)” on one of the pages. I know your birthday is in the spring but I always mix up whether it’s early in the year or closer to the summer term, because you’re forever saying your class makes more of a fuss about it than you do. Can you text me the exact month and year so I don’t guess and have to correct it later?

I’ve already put in that you’re currently unemployed and that your previous occupation was a 3rd class primary school teacher at St. Brigid’s National School, Castleknock. I also mentioned that from September 2022 to May 2024 you were on a reduced timetable (0.6 post) because you were using Wednesday afternoons and Fridays for counselling sessions at the Mater psychology clinic after that horrible assault by the parent outside the school gate.

I’ve tried to explain why we fell behind, but I’m worried I might be over‑sharing. I’ve said part of the problem is that I didn’t adjust my spending quickly enough when things got tighter and I carried on with some non‑essential expenses instead of cutting back straight away.

M.

metadata.subject: Forms for the arrears review – need your DOB

artifacts.content:
From: Emma O'Connor <emma.oconnor@gmail.com>
To: Michael O'Connor <m.oconnor@gmail.com>
Date: 2024-07-03 08:06
Subject: Re: Forms for the arrears review – DOB etc.

Hi love,

Just saw your email before heading out with Aoife and Liam.

First off, no, my birthday is *not* in the summer – you’re mixing me up with Aoife again. Hers is during the last week of the school year, remember? Mine is earlier, when the kids are only just settling back after the Christmas break and everyone is still half asleep. The month is written on the front of that old passport in the top drawer if you really can’t remember.

For the arrears review form, don’t stress too much. As long as the year is right and you spell my name properly, they’re hardly going to throw it back because you’ve muddled the month. But do try to get it right if you can.

I read what you wrote about me being currently unemployed and my previous job as a 3rd class teacher in St. Brigid’s – that’s fine. You can mention the reduced timetable and the counselling at the Mater if it helps them understand, but keep it factual.

By the way, you also forgot to include that one of the missed payments was when your mam was in the Mater for that urgent overnight cardiac observation – the one you had to pay €2,750 up front for. That’s important context.

I’ll dig out the passport this evening and send you the exact month and year.

E x

Subject: Clarification on information requested – account ending 4932

Dear Arrears Support Team,

Further to your recent letter regarding the standard financial statement and supporting documentation for our mortgage account ending 4932, I wanted to clarify one point before returning the forms.

On page 3 you request “Borrower 2 date of birth (month and year)”. My wife, Emma, is currently at home with the children and between school holidays and appointments we keep missing each other to go through the paperwork properly. I know her birth year, but I’m unsure whether you need the exact calendar month or if an approximate indication (e.g. “early in the year”) is acceptable at this stage, given that you already hold full ID on file from when we drew down the mortgage.

I have otherwise completed the sections on her current unemployment and her previous position as a 3rd class primary school teacher at St. Brigid’s National School, Castleknock. Kind regards,

Michael Andrew O’Connor

Home (Castleknock): number not provided  
Next of kin: Michael O’Connor – 086 441 9820

Please confirm if your personal mobile is still 089 335 2049, or reply with the correct number if it has changed. This is the one we use for urgent calls (e.g. if Aoife or Liam becomes unwell, or if there’s a school closure at short notice).

Michael – we’ve kept your mobile on file as 086 441 9820 for emergency contact purposes. If you’d prefer we use your work mobile instead, or if that number has changed, let us know and we’ll amend the list.

Emma, we’re also aware you’ve temporarily reduced to 3.5 days a week from early September to just before Christmas so you can bring Liam to multiple assessments at the Children’s Health Ireland clinic in Temple Street. If there’s an alternative daytime number we should use on the days you’re off (e.g. a different mobile), please include that in your reply.

Kind regards,

Siobhán 
School Secretary

To-do session after kids’ bedtime to sort out admin that slipped during last year.

- Log into AIB online banking and check which contact number is listed for Emma as “Borrower 2 mobile telephone” on the mortgage profile. Pretty sure it’s still showing her old Vodafone number ending in 44 instead of the current one.
- Confirm Emma’s current personal mobile: she’s using 087 942 6610 for everything now (calls, WhatsApp with parents, school contact). Need to make sure this is what all the accounts have.
- Call AIB customer service from landline and request update of her mobile to 087 942 6610 for alerts and verification texts.
- While on, ask them to resend the arrears statement by email – the one from late October never arrived properly.
- Update Electric Ireland, Irish Water, and Virgin Media with Emma’s mobile so she doesn’t keep missing security codes.
- Double‑check that St. Brigid’s has her correct number on the staff list; they may still have the temporary SIM she used when she cut back to 3.5 days a week from early September to just before Christmas 2025 to bring Liam to the assessments in Temple Street.

Notes:

Bring folder with printed letters from AIB and the arrears review paperwork. Have Emma nearby in case they need her verbal authorisation to change the contact number.

artifacts.content:
Title: Call broker – clarify Emma’s mobile on application
Date: 2026-01-09 13:00–13:20
Location: Car park, Park West Business Park
Attendees: Michael O’Connor

Description:

Ring Brian (mortgage broker) during lunch to clear up the confusion about the “Borrower 2 mobile telephone” field on the top-up application.

Points to cover:

- Explain that Emma has had three different mobile numbers in the last few years:  
  1) Old Vodafone number ending 1183 – this might still be what the bank has.  
  2) Temporary prepay SIM she used when she cut back to 3.5 days a week from early September to just before Christmas 2025 for Liam’s Temple Street assessments and asthma check-ups – school had that one for a while.  
  3) Current main mobile: 087 942 6610, which is what she uses now for everything.
- Ask Brian which number he actually needs on the form – the one currently on the bank’s system, or Emma’s current working mobile.
- Confirm that the bank doesn’t mind if the number on the application is different to what’s on their file, as long as we ring and update it before drawdown.
- Check that he’s not expecting a landline for Borrower 2; we don’t use the house phone.

Reminder: sit in the car to make the call so it’s quiet; bring the printed application pack and Emma’s latest payslip in case Brian asks for any details while we’re on the phone.

metadata.title: Call broker – clarify Emma’s mobile on application
metadata.location: Car park, Park West Business Park

artifacts.content:
From: Michael O'Connor <m.oconnor@gmail.com>
To: Emma O'Connor <emma.oconnor@example.com>
Date: 2025-01-18 21:14
Subject: Mortgage review form – your contact details

Hey Em,

Just started filling in the mortgage arrears review pack they sent out. They’re looking for “Borrower 2 mobile telephone” on the second page, so I want to be sure I don’t put in the school’s landline by mistake like last time.

Can you confirm which number you actually want on it? I have your current mobile saved as 087 945 1183, but I know you were talking about switching providers after you finished up at St. Brigid’s. I remember you saying the new SIM might come with a different number and you didn’t want parents from your old 3rd class ringing you on your personal phone anymore.

Some of the questions are fairly detailed. One asks why the arrears started, and I’ve explained that our arrears began after I had to take three weeks of unpaid leave from DigitalConnect Solutions Ltd. in late October 2024 so I could attend daily oncology appointments with Mam at the Mater. Between the lost income (about €1,600) and roughly €450 on medication and parking, we fell behind on the November and December payments.

I’ve also admitted that, to be honest, I buried my head in the sand for a few months and ignored the letters and calls from the bank because dealing with it stressed me out.

Text me back with the mobile number you want on the form and I’ll finish this tonight.

M.

metadata.subject: Mortgage review form – your contact details

artifacts.content:
From: Emma O'Connor <emma.oconnor@example.com>
To: Michael O'Connor <m.oconnor@gmail.com>
Date: 2025-01-19 08:02
Subject: Re: Need your mobile for the bank forms

Hi Mike,

Saw your missed call – was in the shower.

For the “Borrower 2 mobile telephone” bit, don’t put the old school contact sheet number. A lot of the parents from St. Brigid’s still have me down as 085 622 9314 from when I was teaching 3rd class, but that SIM is in the drawer and barely works. Since I’m currently unemployed and not dealing with parents anymore, I’d rather the bank had my personal mobile, not the one that used to be on classroom notices.

As you know, I was working 27.5 hours a week there until June 2024, when I took the voluntary redundancy package of €18,000 after they warned me in April that the post might be suppressed. I don’t want anything linked to the school on the mortgage stuff now.

Use the number that’s on the family WhatsApp group – that’s the one I actually answer. If you can’t find it, check the GP’s file for Liam; I updated it there when we were sorting his asthma meds.

And yes, I agree we need to be upfront this time. Part of the reason we’re in this mess is that we’ve been prioritising other bills and day‑to‑day spending over the mortgage, thinking it was the one thing we could fall behind on temporarily.

Talk later,
Em

metadata.subject: Re: Need your mobile for the bank forms

artifacts.content:
Title: Call with bank – confirm Emma’s mobile
Date: 2025-01-21 13:30–14:00
Location: Quiet room, DigitalConnect Solutions office
Attendees: Michael O’Connor, AIB arrears support

Description:

Agenda:
- Ring AIB arrears support line to go through the Standard Financial Statement
- Double‑check they have the correct contact details for both borrowers
- Specifically confirm Borrower 2 mobile telephone and email address

Notes to self:
- Have Emma’s current mobile number in front of me. She said not to use the old 085 622 9314 one from the school contact lists, or the temporary PAYG number she used when her main phone was in for repair last summer (pretty sure that one started with 089, need to avoid confusion).
- If they ask why arrears started, explain that the arrears started to build after Revenue placed a deduction‑at‑source on my salary in March 2024 for an underpayment of income tax from 2021–2022, so about €320 a month has been taken directly from my wages at DigitalConnect Solutions Ltd., leaving us short for the full mortgage repayment.
- Also be honest that I ignored a lot of their letters and calls because dealing with it was stressing me out.

Prep:
- Bring latest payslips and Emma’s redundancy letter.
- Check that Emma is happy with which mobile number I’m giving them; she mentioned maybe switching back to her old 087 945 1183 if the new provider keeps dropping calls.
- Ask them to send any follow‑up texts to my mobile as well, in case her phone is off when she’s with the kids.

metadata.title: Call with bank – confirm Emma’s mobile

Dear Mr O’Connor,

Further to your telephone conversation with our Arrears Support Unit on 21 January 2025, we require written confirmation of the correct contact details for Borrower 2 on your mortgage account ending ****2841.

Our records currently show the following telephone number for Borrower 2:

Borrower 2 mobile: 085 622 9314

During your call you indicated that this may be an old number associated with your wife’s previous role as a teacher at St. Brigid’s National School, Castleknock, and that she may now prefer to use a different mobile for all mortgage‑related correspondence. Please reply to this email confirming whether the above number is still valid, or advising us of the replacement mobile number for Borrower 2.

As discussed, we have noted your explanation that arrears began after changes to your income and family circumstances, including periods of unpaid leave and additional medical expenses. We appreciate your cooperation in completing the Standard Financial Statement and understand that this can be a stressful process.

Please respond within 7 days so that we can ensure all text message updates regarding your account are sent to the appropriate mobile phone.

Kind regards,

Siobhán Kelly
AIB Arrears Support Unit
